user_prompt: does each callback finalize a SPI transaction?
agent_reply: No. In our current [SpriteDraw.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/SpriteDraw/SpriteDraw.cpp:182), the whole PNG decode is wrapped in one display transaction:
Inside each pngle callback, we only do:
Those do not call `endWrite()`. So the SPI transaction is held open for the whole decode/draw, about your 130 ms.
That is the fastest arrangement for drawing the splash, because it avoids re-acquiring/reconfiguring SPI on every tiny callback. But it also means the SD card cannot use that SPI bus during the draw.
If we wanted SD-friendly drawing, we’d move `startWrite()` / `endWrite()` into each callback or every N lines. That would let SD sneak in between chunks, but the splash would get slower.

user_prompt: do this: for the call parameters of `drawPng`, add a boolean to indicate if I want the fast mode or the slower mode, and an optional callback to be called on during the draw callbacks. So the context struct needs to carry both of those things, and edit the test to include these features
agent_reply: Implemented. `SpriteDraw::drawPng(...)` now takes a runtime `fast_mode` boolean plus an optional per-decode-callback hook:
SpriteDraw::drawPng(..., bool fast_mode, DrawCallback callback = nullptr, void* callback_user_data = nullptr)
Fast mode keeps one SPI transaction open for the whole PNG decode/draw. Slow mode opens/closes the display transaction inside each pngle draw callback.

I also added `DrawCallbackInfo` and updated [test_pngdecode.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_pngdecode.cpp) to pass `kFastPngDraw` plus a small callback that records progress counters. Flip this line to test the slower bus-friendly path:

user_prompt: don't patch the symptoms away, why is the allocation failing? debug print heap size before and after `lgfx_pngle_new`, do not test build
agent_reply: Added heap diagnostics directly around `lgfx_pngle_new()` in [SpriteDraw.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/SpriteDraw/SpriteDraw.cpp:202).
`free8`, `largest8`, `free_internal`, `largest_internal`, and `min_free8` before and after the allocation.

The likely reason: `lgfx_pngle_new()` does one large `malloc(sizeof(pngle_t))`. In M5GFX’s `lgfx_pngle.c`, that object includes a 32 KB LZ buffer plus an inflater noted as about 11 KB, so it needs a large contiguous heap block, not just enough total free heap. Watch `largest8` / `largest_internal`; if those are below roughly 44-46 KB, allocation will fail.
I did not run a build.

commit_message: good progress working on GUI but ran into memory problems with PNG decode

user_prompt: avoid having `M5GFX& display` as a parameter for functions and methods, there should be a global reference to the display for anything unrelated to FlyGui, and if FlyGui is involved, there should be a way to dig through trees to get a reference to it, or just use the global reference
agent_reply: - Updated direct display users to use `thefly_display` instead of threading a local `display` reference or calling `M5.Display` directly.
- Kept `FlyGui::display()` as a convenience getter that returns the global display, so tree-based GUI code can still ask the GUI for its screen if needed.

user_prompt: This will involve `lib\SpriteDraw\SpriteDraw.cpp` `SpriteDraw::drawPng` and `draw_png_run` and `argb_to_rgb565` and maybe others.

I want to add a parameter for darkening or dithering a sprite. I want to be able to draw icons but either darkened or dithered with black, as to indicate "this item is inactive", without having a pile of extra graphic assets.

`SpriteDraw::drawPng` takes this as a parameter, `PngDecodeContext` carries this setting around to `draw_png_run`. `argb_to_rgb565` takes in a new parameter. If dither flag is involved, then right before `thefly_display.drawPixel(pixel_x, dst_y, argb_to_rgb565(argb));`, we think if Y is even, then X must be even, if Y is odd, X must be odd, otherwise draw black.
and fix up any mistakes in that file
agent_reply: - Dither mode draws normal/darkened pixels only when `(x & 1) == (y & 1)`, otherwise black.
- Works in both single-pixel and run/chunk drawing paths.
SpriteDraw::drawPng(data, bytes, x, y, w, h, true,

user_prompt: inside `void draw_png_run(void* user_data, uint32_t x, uint32_t y, uint_fast8_t div_x, size_t length, const uint8_t* argb)`, if the currently drawn `argb` data says the alpha is fully transparent, skip that pixel.
agent_reply: Fully transparent pixels, where `argb[0] == 0`, are now skipped. For normal horizontal runs, I changed the buffered write to emit only contiguous opaque spans so transparent pixels preserve the existing background instead of becoming black.

user_prompt: change this to `considered_transparent_pixel` and set the threshold to 25% of full scale
agent_reply: `fully_transparent_pixel()` is now `considered_transparent_pixel()`, and alpha values `<= 255 / 4` are treated as transparent. I also updated all call sites and normalized the line endings back so the diff stays clean.
Verified with `git diff --check`. No build run.

user_prompt: also make sure every `.cpp` file inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` meet the guidelines at `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md`, however, it is also important that their code layout look almost the same between them as they do kind of inherit from the same base classes sometimes
agent_reply: The common shape is now: constants/types/prototypes first, primary view/API methods next, supporting/drawing/formatting helpers later. I also kept the FlyGui view files visually aligned with each other by using the same lightweight section markers and putting helper blocks at the bottom.
- `src\version.c` build churn was restored; final status only shows the intended layout files.
